coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots modified dibismuth tetraoxide microrods: A direct Z-scheme photocatalyst with excellent visible-light photocatalytic performance
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Yue, Xiaoyang
Miao, Xuli
Ji, Zhenyuan
Shen, Xiaoping
Zhou, Hu
Kong, Lirong
Zhu, Guoxing
Li, Xiaoyun
Ali Shah, Sayyar
coredata.dc:description:
In this work, an all solid direct Z-scheme photocatalyst of monoclinic dibismuth tetraoxide microrods/nitrogen-doped carbon dots (m-Bi2O4/NCDs) has been constructed through a simple one-step hydrothermal method. The m-Bi2O4/NCDs photocatalyst exhibits excellent photocatalytic activity for the degradation of methylene orange (MO) and phenol under visible light irradiation. The pollutants of MO (10 mg L−1) and phenol (45 mg L−1) could be efficiently degraded by m-Bi2O4/NCDs within 30 and 120 min, respectively, which is much better than that of the single m-Bi2O4, indicating that the introduction of NCDs into m-Bi2O4 can effectively improve the photocatalytic activity. Moreover, the m-Bi2O4/NCDs photocatalyst possesses a high photocatalytic stability and durability, and its photocatalytic activity did not show obvious decline after four photodegradation cycles. It is found that both
O2 − and direct h+ oxidation play important roles in the degradation process, and based on the experimental result a direct Z-scheme photocatalytic mechanism is proposed. This study suggests that the as-prepared m-Bi2O4/NCDs composite is a promising photocatalyst for environmental remediation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dibismuth tetraoxide
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Z-scheme photocatalyst
Organic pollutants

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of m-Bi2O4, m-Bi2O4/NCDs-1, m-Bi2O4/NCDs-2, m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 and m-Bi2O4/NCDs-4.
(a-c) TEM images of m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 and (d) the HRTEM of m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3.
FT-IR spectra of the as-prepared m-Bi2O4, m-Bi2O4/NCDs-1, m-Bi2O4/NCDs-2, m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 and m-Bi2O4/NCDs-4.
(a) XPS survey spectrum and high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) O 1 s, (c) Bi 4f and (d) C 1 s for the m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 composite. The inset in Fig. 4a displays the N 1 s peak.

UV–vis DRS of (a) m-Bi2O4, m-Bi2O4/NCDs-1, m-Bi2O4/NCDs-2, m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 and m-Bi2O4/NCDs-4 and (b) NCDs. The insets display the band gap values estimated from the plotted curves of (αhν)r vs hν for r = 1/2 and 2, respectively.
Mott–Schottky plot for NCDs according to impedance measurements. The flat-band potential is obtained from the intercept of the extrapolated line.

The photocatalytic degradation of (a) MO and (b) phenol based on the as-prepared samples; Pseudo-first-order kinetic curves of the photocatalytic degradation of (c) MO and (d) phenol over different photocatalysts; The rate constants of the photodegradation of (e) MO and (f) phenol with different photocatalysts; (g) Cycle experiments of m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 for photocatalytic degradation of MO under visible-light irradiation. (h) XRD patterns of the initial m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 and the m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 after four cycles for photocatalytic degradation of MO.
Transient photocurrent response curves of m-Bi2O4, m-Bi2O4/NCDs-1, m-Bi2O4/NCDs-2, m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 and m-Bi2O4/NCDs-4 photocatalysts.
The trapping experiments of the active species for the photocatalytic reactions based on (a) m-Bi2O4 and (b) m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 photocatalysts.

ESR spectra of (a) DMPO
OH adducts in aqueous solution and (b) DMPO
O2 − adducts in methanol solution recorded with m-Bi2O4 and m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 under visible light irradiation.
The proposed charge-transfer mechanisms of (a) type-I and (b) Z-scheme heterojunctions for m-Bi2O4/NCDs photocatalysts under visible-light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots modified dibismuth tetraoxide microrods: A direct Z-scheme photocatalyst with excellent visible-light photocatalytic performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang 212003, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science and Engineering
Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
In this work, an all solid direct Z-scheme photocatalyst of monoclinic dibismuth tetraoxide microrods/nitrogen-doped carbon dots (m-Bi2O4/NCDs) has been constructed through a simple one-step hydrothermal method. The m-Bi2O4/NCDs photocatalyst exhibits excellent photocatalytic activity for the degradation of methylene orange (MO) and phenol under visible light irradiation. The pollutants of MO (10 mg L−1) and phenol (45 mg L−1) could be efficiently degraded by m-Bi2O4/NCDs within 30 and 120 min, respectively, which is much better than that of the single m-Bi2O4, indicating that the introduction of NCDs into m-Bi2O4 can effectively improve the photocatalytic activity. Moreover, the m-Bi2O4/NCDs photocatalyst possesses a high photocatalytic stability and durability, and its photocatalytic activity did not show obvious decline after four photodegradation cycles. It is found that both
O2 − and direct h+ oxidation play important roles in the degradation process, and based on the experimental result a direct Z-scheme photocatalytic mechanism is proposed. This study suggests that the as-prepared m-Bi2O4/NCDs composite is a promising photocatalyst for environmental remediation.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, the increasing amount of sewage discharged by industries has seriously affected the health of human beings. Therefore, how to deal with contaminants in wastewater in an effective and environment-friendly way has become the hotspot of current research. As early as 1976, TiO2 photocatalyst was first applied to photocatalytic degradation of pollutants for environmental remediation [1]. After that lots of semiconductor photocatalysts were deeply researched and developed, such as ZnO [2], CdS [3], BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I) [4], Ag3PO4 [5], etc. However, the practical applications of these single-component semiconductors were limited due to their low visible-light utilization and the high recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Hence, to develop new semiconductor photocatalysts with a suitable bandgap and improved photocatalytic performance has been the research focus in the field ofphotocatalysis. Recently, some Bi-based compounds have attracted a large amount of attention as visible light-driven photocatalysts. Among these compounds, Bi element is presented in the form of trivalent and pentavalent states. It was considered that the hybridized O 2p and Bi 6 s2 valence bands in Bi3+-containing oxide resulted in its narrow bandgap together with the visible light response activity, while the empty 6 s orbital of Bi5+ also supported high visible light photocatalytic activity for Bi5+-containing compounds [6]. Very recently, Wang et al. [7] reported a new visible-light-driven Bi-based photocatalyst of monoclinic dibismuth tetraoxide (m-Bi2O4). This simple oxide with mixed valent states (Bi3+ and Bi5+) has a narrow bandgap of only 2.0 eV, and is responsive to light wavelength up to 620 nm. However, the photocatalytic efficiency of pristine m-Bi2O4 is still not high enough for practical applications. Since the construction of semiconductor heterojunctions is an effective way to improve photocatalytic efficiency [8], several m-Bi2O4-based heterojunctions have been reported. For example, Wang et al. [9] reported g-C3N4/Bi2O4 heterojunction photocatalyst with enhanced photocatalytic performance and photochemical stability. Xia et al. [10] reported a superparamagnetic Bi2O4/Fe3O4 photocatalyst with enhanced visible-light photocatalytic performance for ibuprofen removal. Sun et al. [11] reported that Bi2O2CO3/Bi2O4 photocatalyst exhibited enhanced visible light photocatalytic performance for phenol degradation. All these researches demonstrated that the photocatalytic efficiency of Bi2O4 can be significantly improved by constructing semiconductor heterostructures.

Recently, carbon dots (CDs), a new zero-dimensional carbon material with a carbon particle size less than 10 nm, have aroused great interest in the scientific community due to their excellent chemical stability, non-toxicity, low cost, up-conversion fluorescence property and excellent electron-transfer and electron-reservoir properties [12–14]. Based on these characteristics, CDs have been used as co-catalyst for the construction of electron surface separation centers and to restrict the recombination of the charge carriers of photocatalysts. At present, many CDs-modified photocatalysts have been developed such as CDs/TiO2 [15], CDs/SiO2 [16], CDs/BiOX (X = Cl, Br and I) [17], g-C3N4/CDs [18], CDs/ZnO [19] and CDs/CuWO4 [20]. Meanwhile, the performance of CDs can be further optimized by doping with other elements such as N [13,21] and S [22], because the doping can significantly affect the electron structure of CDs and provide a new route for the design of photocatalysts with visible or even near infrared region (NIR) responses [23]. Generally, CDs were used as co-catalyst in the above-reported photocatalysts. However, some recent studies demonstrated that CDs can also act as a semiconductor in photocatalytic processes. For instance, Yang et al. [24] reported that under ultraviolet light irradiation, the pure carbon dots also exhibit photocatalytic activity towards Rhodamine B (RhB) degradation. Ma et al. [13] reported that MO could be well degraded by N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) under visible light irradiation.

In recent years, the heterogeneous Z-scheme photocatalysts have attracted intense attention owing to its unique advantages [25]. In a heterogeneous Z-scheme photocatalytic system, the photogenerated electrons and holes will have an efficient separation due to the recombination of photogenerated electrons from the lower CB and the photogenerated holes from the higher VB, resulting in high charge-separation efficiency and stronger redox ability. Based on the type of the employed electron mediators, the Z-scheme photocatalytic systems can be divided into three types: PS-A/D-PS, PS-PS and PS-C-PS (PS: photosystem, A/D: electron acceptor/donor and C: conductor as the electron mediator) [26]. Among them, the two-component PS-PS systems have many advantages such as the shortened path of Z-scheme electron transfer and the easier preparation methods, indicating a great potential in practical applications. In this paper, an all solid direct Z-scheme m-Bi2O4/NCDs photocatalyst has been prepared by a simple hydrothermal method and its photocatalytic property is evaluated by the degradation of methylene orange (MO) and phenol as model pollutants.

The reagents used in this experiment were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd., all of which are of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. The water used in the experiment is deionized (DI) water. The NCDs were prepared by a previously reported method [27–28].

In a typical synthesis, 0.53 g of NaBiO3 was mixed with a certain volume of NCDs aqueous dispersion (1 mg mL−1), afterwards DI water was added in to make the total volume reach to 20 mL. The resulting mixture was stirred for 24 h, and then transferred into a 30 mL Teflon lined stainless autoclave and heated at 160 ◦C for 8 h. After collected by centrifugation and washed three times with DI water and ethanol, respectively, the product was put into the vacuum drying at 45 ◦C for 12 h. The obtained samples were denoted as m-Bi2O4/NCDs-1, m-Bi2O4/NCDs-2, m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 and m-Bi2O4/NCDs-4 for the NCDs feeding amount of 0.8, 1.6, 4 and 8 mg, respectively. For comparison, pristine m-Bi2O4 was prepared under the same conditions but without the addition of NCDs.

The phase structures of the as-synthesized samples were investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 ADVANCE) with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) at a scanning rate of 7° min−1. The morphologies, sizes and microstructures of the samples were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI spectrometer. The UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra were measured in the range of 200–1000 nm using an Ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (UV–vis DRS, Shimadzu UV-2450, Japan) with BaSO4 as a reflectance standard material. The infrared absorption spectra of the as-prepared samples were recorded by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Nicolet Nexus 470). The electron spin resonance (ESR) signals of radicals spin-trapped by spin-trap reagent 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) were examined on a Bruker EPR A300-10/12 spectrometer. Thermogravimetric analysis (TG) was conducted on an integrated thermal analyzer (NETZSCH STA449C) at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 in air.

MO and phenol were chosen as the target contaminants for photocatalytic degradation to evaluate the photocatalytic activities of the as-prepared samples. The photodegradation experiments were conducted on a GHX-3 photochemical reactor (Science and Education Equipment Co., Ltd, Yangzhou, China). A 250 W Xe lamp with a 420 nm cutoff filter was used as the visible light source. In a typical experiment for MO degradation, 30 mg of the as-prepared photocatalyst was dispersed in 60 mL of MO aqueous solution (10 mg L−1). While for the degradation of phenol, the dosage of the catalyst is 50 mg and it was dispersed in 50 mL of phenol aqueous solution (45 mg L−1). All the experiments were carried out under constant stirring at room temperature. Prior to irradiation, the solution was magnetically stirred for 1 h in the dark to reach an adsorption-desorption equilibrium between the photocatalyst and the contaminant. During the photocatalytic process, regular intervals were recorded to withdraw 3 mL of the suspension, which was then centrifuged. The resulted supernatant was analyzed to determine the residual concentration of the pollutant by an UV–vis spectrophotometer. The characteristic absorption wavelengths of MO and phenol are 464 nm and 270 nm, respectively. In the recycle experiment, the samples collected by centrifugation and further washed with ethanol and deionized water were re-dispersed into fresh pollutant solution for next cycle.

Electrochemical measurements were performed on a 760D electrochemical analyzer (ChenHua Instruments, Shanghai, China) using a standard three-electrode system. The platinum wire and saturated calomel electrode (SCE) were used as counter electrodes and reference electrodes, respectively. 0.1 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution was used as the electrolyte. The working electrode was a FTO glass deposited with the as-prepared sample with a light irradiation area of 1.0 cm2. In detail, the working electrode was prepared as follows: First, 4 mg of the as-prepared samples was dispersed in a mixed solution of 980 μL of ethanol and 20 μL of Nafion by sonication to form a homogeneous suspension, afterwards 0.1 mL of the above suspension was coated on an FTO glass plate and dried overnight at 45 °C to ensure that the samples were tightly attached on the FTO. A 300 W Xe lamp was utilized as the light source, and the distance between the illumination light source and the photocathode was fixed at 25 cm. The Mott–Schottky plot was obtained from the electrochemical impedance measurement, and the potential ranged from −2.0 to 0.5 V (vs. SCE) with the frequency settled at 1.0 kHz.

Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns of the as-synthesized m-Bi2O4, m-Bi2O4/NCDs-1, m-Bi2O4/NCDs-2, m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 and m-Bi2O4/NCDs-4. For the pure Bi2O4, all the diffraction peaks can be indexed to the standard card of monoclinic Bi2O4 (JCPDS No. 83–0410), which is a mixed valent bismuth oxide with the formula of Bi3+Bi5+O4 [7]. The m-Bi2O4/NCDs composites show similar XRD patterns with pure Bi2O4, and no diffraction peaks from the carbon dots can be observed. This may be ascribed to the low content and the poor crystallinity of NCDs compared to m-Bi2O4, as revealed by the wide diffraction peak of pure NCDs (Fig. 1).

The elemental analysis (Table S1, SI) revealed that C and N are the main elements of NCDs. Raman spectrum of NCDs (Fig. S1, SI) shows two prominent peaks at 1370 and 1577 cm−1, corresponding to the D and G bands of carbon, respectively. XPS survey spectrum verified the presence of the C, N, O elements (Fig. S2a, SI). The high resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. S2b, SI) revealed that there are three different types of carbon atoms: graphitic or aliphatic (CC and CC), oxygenated and nitrous carbon, corresponding to the three deconvoluted peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.2 eV and 288.0 eV, respectively, which is in good consistent with the literature report [27]. TEM image (Fig. S3a, SI) revealed that the size of the as-prepared NCDs is approximately 3–5 nm. These results indicate the successful preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon dots. The pure m-Bi2O4 exhibits rod-like morphology with a diameter ranging from 300 nm to 1 μm (Fig. S3b, SI). Fig. 2 shows the TEM images of m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3. It can be seen that m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 shows similar rod-like morphology as m-Bi2O4, and the diameter of the m-Bi2O4 microrods shows no obvious change after the addition of NCDs. And some tiny nanoparticles with a size of several nanometers are anchored on the surface of the m-Bi2O4 microrods, confirming the presence of NCDs. In addition, the clear lattice fringes with the spacings of 0.331 and 0.275 nm correspond to the (1 1 1) and ( −2 0 2) lattice planes of m-Bi2O4, respectively, further confirming the formation of m-Bi2O4 (Fig. 2d).
Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2018.07.086.

The elemental analysis (Table S1 , SI) revealed that C and N are the main elements of NCDs. Raman spectrum of NCDs (Fig. S1, SI) shows two prominent peaks at 1370 and 1577 cm−1, corresponding to the D and G bands of carbon, respectively. XPS survey spectrum verified the presence of the C, N, O elements (Fig. S2a, SI). The high resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. S2b, SI) revealed that there are three different types of carbon atoms: graphitic or aliphatic (CC and CC), oxygenated and nitrous carbon, corresponding to the three deconvoluted peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.2 eV and 288.0 eV, respectively, which is in good consistent with the literature report [27]. TEM image (Fig. S3a, SI) revealed that the size of the as-prepared NCDs is approximately 3–5 nm. These results indicate the successful preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon dots. The pure m-Bi2O4 exhibits rod-like morphology with a diameter ranging from 300 nm to 1 μm (Fig. S3b, SI). Fig. 2 shows the TEM images of m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3. It can be seen that m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 shows similar rod-like morphology as m-Bi2O4, and the diameter of the m-Bi2O4 microrods shows no obvious change after the addition of NCDs. And some tiny nanoparticles with a size of several nanometers are anchored on the surface of the m-Bi2O4 microrods, confirming the presence of NCDs. In addition, the clear lattice fringes with the spacings of 0.331 and 0.275 nm correspond to the (1 1 1) and ( −2 0 2) lattice planes of m-Bi2O4, respectively, further confirming the formation of m-Bi2O4 (Fig. 2d).

The FT-IR spectra of the as-prepared samples are shown in Fig. 3 . The peaks at 549, 588 and 631 cm−1 for the pristine m-Bi2O4 can be ascribed to the stretching vibrations of the BiO bond in m-Bi2O4, which is in good consistent with the previous report [9]. In the FT-IR spectrum of the NCDs, the wide absorption band at around 3388 cm−1 originates from the stretching vibration of OH, which can be ascribed to -OH functional groups on the surface of the carbon dots and/or the adsorbed H2O molecules. The absorption peaks at 2969 cm−1 (υC-H), 1650 cm−1 (υC=O), 1558 cm−1 (δN-H), 1396 (υC-N) and 1050 (υC-O-C) are also clearly observed, which are consistent with the characteristic absorptions of the NCDs reported previously [29]. For the m-Bi2O4/NCDs composites, besides the characteristic peaks from m-Bi2O4, the characteristic peaks of NCDs become gradually protruding with the increasing content of NCDs, indicating that NCDs are successfully combined with m-Bi2O4.

To investigate the exact compositions of the as-prepared samples, the thermogravimetric (TG) analysis was performed, and the results are shown in Fig. S4 (SI). The m-Bi2O4 shows two continuous weight loss steps at 250–300 °C and 300–350 °C, which correspond to the conversions from Bi2O4 to Bi4O7 and further from Bi4O7 to Bi2O3, respectively [30]. No obvious weight loss can be observed in the temperature range of 350–500 °C. Bi2O4/NCDs-3 exhibits a similar TG curve with m-Bi2O4 owing to the low content of NCDs. The weight loss at 400 °C is approximately 3.7 and 3.3 wt% for Bi2O4/NCDs-3 and m-Bi2O4, respectively. The difference of about 0.4 wt% could be ascribed to the presence of NCDs in the binary composite of m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3.

To investigate the exact compositions of the as-prepared samples, the thermogravimetric (TG) analysis was performed, and the results are shown in Fig. S4 (SI). The m-Bi2O4 shows two continuous weight loss steps at 250–300 °C and 300–350 °C, which correspond to the conversions from Bi2O4 to Bi4O7 and further from Bi4O7 to Bi2O3, respectively [30]. No obvious weight loss can be observed in the temperature range of 350–500 °C. Bi2O4/NCDs-3 exhibits a similar TG curve with m-Bi2O4 owing to the low content of NCDs. The weight loss at 400 °C is approximately 3.7 and 3.3 wt% for Bi2O4/NCDs-3 and m-Bi2O4, respectively. The difference of about 0.4 wt% could be ascribed to the presence of NCDs in the binary composite of m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3.

XPS measurement was further performed to investigate the surface composition and chemical states of the m-Bi2O4/NCDs samples. From the XPS survey spectrum in Fig. 4 a, the elements of C, N, O and Bi were detected in Bi2O4/NCDs-3, which is in good consistent with the composition of the composite. Fig. 4b shows the high-resolution O 1 s spectrum, and the three deconvoluted peaks at 529.4, 530.5 and 532.5 eV can be ascribed to BiO, OH and CO bonds, respectively. The BiO bond corresponds to the O in m-Bi2O4, the OH bond is due to the -OH functional groups of the NCDs and/or the adsorbed H2O molecules on the surface of the sample, and the CO peak arises from the oxygen-containing groups in NCDs. The high-resolution Bi 4f spectrum is showed in Fig. 4c, the peaks of Bi 4f7/2 and 4f5/2 can be well deconvoluted into two bimodal peaks at binding energies of 158.5 and 159.3 eV for Bi 4f7/2, and 163.8 and 164.5 eV for Bi 4f5/2, further confirming the presence of Bi(III) and Bi(V) in m-Bi2O4 [7,31]. In C1s spectrum, the deconvoluted peaks at 284.8, 286.5 and 288.4 eV can be ascribed to CC, CO and NCN bonds, respectively. N1s spectrum (the inset in Fig. 4a) shows a very weak peak at around 400 eV, which can be ascribed to the low content of N element. These results further prove that the NCDs have been successfully anchored on the surface of m-Bi2O4. Moreover, the energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) (Fig. S5, SI) further confirms the existence of C, N, O, Bi elements in the m-Bi2O4/NCDs, which is consistent with the XPS results.

XPS measurement was further performed to investigate the surface composition and chemical states of the m-Bi2O4/NCDs samples. From the XPS survey spectrum in Fig. 4 a, the elements of C, N, O and Bi were detected in Bi2O4/NCDs-3, which is in good consistent with the composition of the composite. Fig. 4b shows the high-resolution O 1 s spectrum, and the three deconvoluted peaks at 529.4, 530.5 and 532.5 eV can be ascribed to BiO, OH and CO bonds, respectively. The BiO bond corresponds to the O in m-Bi2O4, the OH bond is due to the -OH functional groups of the NCDs and/or the adsorbed H2O molecules on the surface of the sample, and the CO peak arises from the oxygen-containing groups in NCDs. The high-resolution Bi 4f spectrum is showed in Fig. 4c, the peaks of Bi 4f7/2 and 4f5/2 can be well deconvoluted into two bimodal peaks at binding energies of 158.5 and 159.3 eV for Bi 4f7/2, and 163.8 and 164.5 eV for Bi 4f5/2, further confirming the presence of Bi(III) and Bi(V) in m-Bi2O4 [7,31]. In C1s spectrum, the deconvoluted peaks at 284.8, 286.5 and 288.4 eV can be ascribed to CC, CO and NCN bonds, respectively. N1s spectrum (the inset in Fig. 4a) shows a very weak peak at around 400 eV, which can be ascribed to the low content of N element. These results further prove that the NCDs have been successfully anchored on the surface of m-Bi2O4. Moreover, the energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) (Fig. S5, SI) further confirms the existence of C, N, O, Bi elements in the m-Bi2O4/NCDs, which is consistent with the XPS results.

The UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the as-prepared samples are depicted in Fig. 5 a. For the pristine m-Bi2O4, an absorption edge at about 600 nm is presented, indicating that the band gap of m-Bi2O4 is about 2.10 eV, which is consistent with previous report [7]. Compared with the pure m-Bi2O4, the m-Bi2O4/NCDs composite shows an obviously enhanced absorption in the region of 200–600 nm, indicating that NCDs play an important role in harvesting visible light. This is mainly due to the intrinsic optical absorbance of the NCDs endowed with their quantum effect [18,32]. Although NCDs has an intrinsic optical absorbance, too high loading amount of NCDs could weaken the light absorption of m-Bi2O4, and unavoidably restrict the efficiency of NCDs in promoting the final optical absorbance of the composites [33]. In order to study the role of NCDs, the UV–vis DRS of NCDs is also measured and shown in Fig. 5b. NCDs show an absorption peak at ca. 350 nm and a strong absorption below 300 nm, which can be ascribed to the n-π* transition of C = X (X = O, N) and π-π* transition of CC [34]. In addition, the optical band gaps (E g) of the m-Bi2O4 and NCDs were calculated based on the formula: (αhν) r = k (hν − E g), where α, h and ν are the absorption coefficient, Planck constant and light frequency, respectively, k is a constant relative to the material and r = 2 as a direct transition semiconductor and r = 1/2 as an indirect transition semiconductor [35]. For pure m-Bi2O4, the E g value is determined to be 2.10 eV, and for the NCDs the E g values are obtained to be 4.45 eV as a direct transition semiconductor and 3.18 eV as an indirect transition semiconductor.

In addition, the band edge positions of valence-band maximum (VBM) and conduction-band minimum (CBM) are crucial to the photocatalytic performance of a semiconductor. The CBM of m-Bi2O4 has been reported to be −0.37 eV [7,31]. The CBM of NCDs can be determined by Mott–Schottky plot. As shown in Fig. 6 , the positive slope of Mott–Schottky plot indicates that NCDs is an n-type semiconductor. Therefore, the donor density follows the Mott–Schottky equation [36]:
1
C
2
=
2
e
N d
ε 0
ε
V −
V fb
Where C is the capacitance (F), N d is the donor density (cm−3), e is the electronic charge unit, ε 0 is the permittivity of the vacuum, ε is the dielectric constant, V is the applied potential (V), and V fb is the flat-band potential (V). As can be seen that when 1/C 2 is zero, the applied potential is equal to the flat band potential V fb. The V fb is determined to be about −1.82 V vs. SCE at pH = 7 (equivalent to −1.58 V vs. NHE at pH = 7). Since the CBM in many n-type semiconductors is more negative by approximate −0.1 V than the V fb [37], the CBM of NCDs is estimated to be about −1.68 V vs. NHE at pH = 7. As shown above, the Eg value of m-Bi2O4 is 2.10 eV, and for the NCDs, the E g values are 4.45 eV for direct transition and 3.18 eV for indirect transition. According to the formula E CB = E VB − Eg, the VB value of m-Bi2O4 is calculated to be 1.72 eV, and the VB value of NCDs is 2.77 eV for direct transition and 1.50 eV for indirect transition, respectively.

MO and phenol were chosen as the model contaminants to evaluate the photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared samples under visible light irradiation. Prior to irradiation, the reaction mixture was stirred in the dark for 60 min to achieve an adsorption-desorption equilibrium between the contaminant and photocatalyst. As shown in Fig. 7 a, after 30 min visible-light irradiation, the degradation efficiencies of MO over P25 (commercial TiO2 photocatalyst), m-Bi2O4, m-Bi2O4/NCDs-1, m-Bi2O4/NCDs-2, m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 and m-Bi2O4/NCDs-4 photocatalysts are 8.64%, 79.7%, 86.8%, 94.9%, 97.4% and 94.3%, respectively. The results show that the introduction of NCDs can significantly improve the photocatalytic performance of the m-Bi2O4. The composite of m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 shows the highest photocatalytic activity. Meanwhile, the photocatalytic properties of our prepared photocatalsyts towards phenol degradation were also evaluated (Fig. 7b). After 120 min visible light irradiation, the degradation efficiency of pure m-Bi2O4 is 70.4%, while the efficiencies of m-Bi2O4/NCDs-1, m-Bi2O4/NCDs-2, m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3, and m-Bi2O4/NCDs-4 are 89.5%, 91.3%, 96.8% and 73.9%, respectively. Obviously, with the increasing content of NCDs, the photocatalytic performance of m-Bi2O4/NCDs composites increases first and then decrease. From the perspective of the mechanism of the Z-scheme photocatalysts, the photogenerated electrons from the CB of PS II combine with the same quantity of photogenerated holes from the VB of PS I at the solid-solid contact interface. When the photogenerated charge carriers in one photosystem exceeds that in another system, the surplus number of these charge carriers probably come across recombination in the bulk and thus the Z-scheme photocatalytic reaction could not be built. Therefore, the ideal situation could be achieved by optimizing the mass (molar) ratio of PS II and PS I, making that PS II and PS I can produce the same number of photogenerated charge carriers [26]. From this perspective and some previous reports [38–39], the excess of either component may lead to the decline of the photocatalytic performance. Therefore, an appropriate amount of NCDs on m-Bi2O4 is important to improve the photocatalytic performance. As shown in Fig. 6c and d, the photocatalytic reaction rate can be described by the pseudo-first-order kinetic: –ln(C/C0) = kt, where t (min) is the irradiation time and k (min−1) is the apparent reaction rate constant. The effect of NCDs content on the photocatalytic activity of the composites is also directly reflected by the apparent rate constants in Fig. 7e and 7f. It can be seen that for the degradation of MO, m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 possesses the highest rate constant (0.12458 min−1), which is about 2.3 times higher than that of m-Bi2O4 (0.05368 min−1). Similarly, for the degradation of phenol, the m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 photocatalyst also possesses the highest rate constant (0.02897 min−1), which is about 2.8 times higher than that of m-Bi2O4 (0.01036 min−1). Obviously, an appropriate amount of NCDs is crucial for achieving the optimum photocatalytic efficiency. The stability of photocatalyst is of great significance to its practical application. The photocatalytic stability of m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 was evaluated by four successive cycles of MO degradation. As shown in Fig. 6g, the degradation efficiency of m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 shows only a slight decrease from 97.4% to 90.5% after four photocatalytic cycles, indicating that the m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 photocatalyst possesses good photocatalytic stability. And the XRD pattern of m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 photocatalyst after four photocatalytic cycles is shown in Fig. 6h, which shows the same diffraction peaks as the initial sample. Furthermore, TEM images of m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 after four photocatalytic cycles are shown in Fig. S6. The m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 photocatalyst after four photocatalytic cycles exhibits almost the same rod-like morphology as the initial one. Thus, both the XRD and TEM results further verify that the m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 photocatalyst possesses a good photocatalytic stability.

MO and phenol were chosen as the model contaminants to evaluate the photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared samples under visible light irradiation. Prior to irradiation, the reaction mixture was stirred in the dark for 60 min to achieve an adsorption-desorption equilibrium between the contaminant and photocatalyst. As shown in Fig. 7 a, after 30 min visible-light irradiation, the degradation efficiencies of MO over P25 (commercial TiO2 photocatalyst), m-Bi2O4, m-Bi2O4/NCDs-1, m-Bi2O4/NCDs-2, m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 and m-Bi2O4/NCDs-4 photocatalysts are 8.64%, 79.7%, 86.8%, 94.9%, 97.4% and 94.3%, respectively. The results show that the introduction of NCDs can significantly improve the photocatalytic performance of the m-Bi2O4. The composite of m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 shows the highest photocatalytic activity. Meanwhile, the photocatalytic properties of our prepared photocatalsyts towards phenol degradation were also evaluated (Fig. 7b). After 120 min visible light irradiation, the degradation efficiency of pure m-Bi2O4 is 70.4%, while the efficiencies of m-Bi2O4/NCDs-1, m-Bi2O4/NCDs-2, m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3, and m-Bi2O4/NCDs-4 are 89.5%, 91.3%, 96.8% and 73.9%, respectively. Obviously, with the increasing content of NCDs, the photocatalytic performance of m-Bi2O4/NCDs composites increases first and then decrease. From the perspective of the mechanism of the Z-scheme photocatalysts, the photogenerated electrons from the CB of PS II combine with the same quantity of photogenerated holes from the VB of PS I at the solid-solid contact interface. When the photogenerated charge carriers in one photosystem exceeds that in another system, the surplus number of these charge carriers probably come across recombination in the bulk and thus the Z-scheme photocatalytic reaction could not be built. Therefore, the ideal situation could be achieved by optimizing the mass (molar) ratio of PS II and PS I, making that PS II and PS I can produce the same number of photogenerated charge carriers [26]. From this perspective and some previous reports [38–39], the excess of either component may lead to the decline of the photocatalytic performance. Therefore, an appropriate amount of NCDs on m-Bi2O4 is important to improve the photocatalytic performance. As shown in Fig. 6c and d, the photocatalytic reaction rate can be described by the pseudo-first-order kinetic: –ln(C/C0) = kt, where t (min) is the irradiation time and k (min−1) is the apparent reaction rate constant. The effect of NCDs content on the photocatalytic activity of the composites is also directly reflected by the apparent rate constants in Fig. 7e and 7f. It can be seen that for the degradation of MO, m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 possesses the highest rate constant (0.12458 min−1), which is about 2.3 times higher than that of m-Bi2O4 (0.05368 min−1). Similarly, for the degradation of phenol, the m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 photocatalyst also possesses the highest rate constant (0.02897 min−1), which is about 2.8 times higher than that of m-Bi2O4 (0.01036 min−1). Obviously, an appropriate amount of NCDs is crucial for achieving the optimum photocatalytic efficiency. The stability of photocatalyst is of great significance to its practical application. The photocatalytic stability of m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 was evaluated by four successive cycles of MO degradation. As shown in Fig. 6g, the degradation efficiency of m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 shows only a slight decrease from 97.4% to 90.5% after four photocatalytic cycles, indicating that the m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 photocatalyst possesses good photocatalytic stability. And the XRD pattern of m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 photocatalyst after four photocatalytic cycles is shown in Fig. 6h, which shows the same diffraction peaks as the initial sample. Furthermore, TEM images of m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 after four photocatalytic cycles are shown in Fig. S6. The m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 photocatalyst after four photocatalytic cycles exhibits almost the same rod-like morphology as the initial one. Thus, both the XRD and TEM results further verify that the m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 photocatalyst possesses a good photocatalytic stability.

The interfacial charge transfer and recombination rates of the as-prepared photocatalysts were studied by the photoelectrochemical analysis. Fig. 8 shows the transient photocurrent responses of m-Bi2O4 and m-Bi2O4/NCDs photocatalysts under intermittent visible-light irradiation for five on-off cycles. Obviously, the photocurrent responses of all binary samples are enhanced, indicating that the introduction of NCDs can significantly enhance the charge carrier density of the m-Bi2O4/NCDs composites under intermittent visible-light irradiation. The m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 presents the highest photocurrent response (0.36 μA cm−2), which is about 3 times of pure m-Bi2O4 (0.12 μA cm−2). However, the photocurrent responses did not increase when the NCDs amount in the composites further increased, which is consistent with the Z-scheme photocatalytic mechanism. The result indicates that the m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 composite possesses much better separation efficiency for the photoinduced charge carriers than the pure m-Bi2O4 and other m-Bi2O4/NCDs composites, demonstrating that an appropriate amount of NCDs can efficiently boost the interfacial charge transfer and separation.

It is known that
O2 −, h+ and
OH are three main active species in photocatalytic process. To clarify the dominant active species in the photocatalytic reactions, the trapping experiments were carried out. Benzoquinone (BZQ), disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate (Na2-EDTA) and isopropanol (IPA) were employed to act as
O2 −, h+ and
OH scavengers, respectively. Fig. 9 a shows the photodegradation of MO with pure m-Bi2O4 photocatalyst after adding various trapping scavengers. It can be seen that the introduction of IPA into the reaction system could hardly affect the photodegradation efficiency, while the introduction of Na2-EDTA almost completely inhibited the MO degradation. In addition, BZQ also has an obvious effect on the degradation efficiency. These results mean that h+ is the main reactive species and
O2 − also plays some role during the photocatalytic process. For m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 photocatalyst, it can be seen from Fig. 9b that h+ still acts as the dominant reactive species, while
O2 − plays a significantly enhanced role compared to that of pure m-Bi2O4, indicating that the introduction of NCDs into the photocatalytic system can largely improve the amount of
O2 − species in the photocatalytic process. As a semiconductor, the CB of NCDs is much more negative than that of m-Bi2O4, while the amount of
O2 − produced by the binary m-Bi2O4/NCDs composite is much more than that of m-Bi2O4 alone, indicating that the electrons in NCDs play a more important role than those in m-Bi2O4, which is consistent with the formation of Z-scheme heterojunction between m-Bi2O4 and NCDs.

In order to further clarify the photocatalytic mechanism, the ESR measurements of the m-Bi2O4 and m-Bi2O4/NCDs were carried out. As shown in Fig. 10 a, no signal of DMPO
OH was detected in the dark. When exposed to visible light irradiation, a very weak ESR signal of DMPO-
OH could be observed over m-Bi2O4 and m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 with almost the same intensity, confirming that
OH species plays little role in the photocatalytic processes of these catalysts. As showed in Fig. 10b, no signal of DMPO-
O2 − was found in the dark in the presence of m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3, proving that no
O2 − radicals were generated in this condition. In contrast, the four characteristic peaks of the DMPO-
O2 − adducts were observed over m-Bi2O4 and m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 under visible light irradiation. Moreover, the DMPO-
O2 − peaks over m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 are much stronger than those over m-Bi2O4, which reveals that more
O2 − radicals were generated over m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 than m-Bi2O4 [40]. Since the
O2 − is generated via a one-electron reduction of O2, the higher
O2 − intensity of m-Bi2O4/NCDs-3 indicates that the enhanced molecular oxygen activation ability can be achieved by the introduction of NCDs. These results are well consistent with those of the trapping experiments.

On the basis of the above results, a possible charge-transfer mechanism for the m-Bi2O4/NCDs photocatalyst is presented in Fig. 11 . As mentioned above, the m-Bi2O4 has a band gap of 2.09 eV, and the CB of m-Bi2O4 is about −0.37 eV according to previous reports [7,31]. As a result, the VB of m-Bi2O4 is estimated to be 1.72 eV. Thus, the potential of electrons produced on m-Bi2O4 is about −0.37 eV, which is only slightly more negative than −0.33 eV to reduce O2 into
O2 −. Therefore,
O2 − plays only a secondary role in the photocatalytic process of pure m-Bi2O4 (Fig. 9a). For the m-Bi2O4/NCDs photocatalyst, if the NCDs underwent a direct transition, its valence band potential is calculated to be 2.77 eV. As shown in Fig. 11a, m-Bi2O4 and NCDs would form type-I heterojunction, which will reduce the photocatalytic performance. Obviously, this is not in accordance with the experimental result. On the contrary, if NCDs undergo an indirect transition, its valence band potential is calculated to be 1.50 eV. The photogenerated electrons in the CB of m-Bi2O4 could combine with the holes on the valence band of NCDs, thus leading to the accumulation of electrons in the CB of NCDs and holes in the VB of m-Bi2O4, which is the typical charge-transfer mode of Z-scheme heterojunctions [26]. Then holes in the VB of m-Bi2O4 would react with pollutants directly, and the electrons in the CB of NCDs with a potential of −1.68 V could more effectively reduce O2 into
O2 −. This is consistent with the enhanced role of
O2 − as demonstrated by the trapping experiments of active species and the ESR results. Therefore, it is reasonably proposed that the m-Bi2O4/NCDs composite is a Z-scheme photocatalyst, in which the effective spatial separation of electron-hole pairs and the more negative conduction band potential of NCDs result in the improvement of the photocatalytic performance.

A new all solid Z-scheme photocatalyst of m-Bi2O4/NCDs, in which m-Bi2O4 microrods are well modified by nitrogen-doped carbon dots, has been successfully prepared by a simple one-step hydrothermal method. The m-Bi2O4/NCDs photocatalyst exhibits significantly enhanced visible-light-driven photocatalytic activities and high photocatalytic stability for MO and phenol degradation. The enhanced photocatalytic performance is ascribed to the enhanced light harvesting capacity and the formation of Z-scheme heterojunctions between m-Bi2O4 and NCDs, which promotes the effective separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs and enhances the molecular oxygen activation ability. It is worth noting that in this study, the NCDs unusually act as a semiconductor instead of a common co-catalyst, demonstrating the versatility of NCDs in photocatalysis. This study demonstrates that Bi2O4 is a potential candidate for visible-light photocatalyst, and the construction of semiconductor heterojunctions between NCDs and oxide semiconductors could be a promising approach to high-performance photocatalysts.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51602129 and 51272094), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu province (No. BK20171295 and BK20150507), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2015M580392 and 2017T100332), and the Jiangsu Province Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 1501025B, 1601231C).

One-step catalase controllable degradation of their bioimaging applications

Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots with oxygen-rich functional groups
Intramolecular hydrogen bonds quench photoluminescence and enhance photocatalytic activity of carbon nanodots
All-solid-state Z-scheme in CdS-Au-TiO2 three-component nanojunction system
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots modified Ag3PO4/GO photocatalyst with excellent visible-light-driven photocatalytic performance and mechanism insight
Facile synthesis of N-doped carbon dots/gC3N4 photocatalyst with enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activity for the degradation of indomethacin

Toward improving the graphene–semiconductor composite photoactivity via the addition of metal ions as generic interfacial mediator
Red emissive sulfur, nitrogen codoped carbon dots and their application in ion detection and theraonostics

Photoelectrochemical study on charge transfer properties of TiO2−B nanowires with an application as humidity sensors
Photocatalytic activity and photoelectric performance enhancement for ZnWO4 by fluorine substitution
Solar water splitting using powdered photocatalysts driven by Z-schematic interparticle electron transfer without an electron mediator
